title: Black Tea on Wool
timeEstimate: Pro-lane within hours; one home round max
stainChemistry: Black tea is the high-tannin variant — more oxidation than green tea, harder stain. The tannin grips wool fast. Wool failures stack: heat felts wool AND sets tannin, alkaline cleaners felt wool AND darken the tannin permanently, agitation felts wet wool on contact. Black tea oxidizes faster than coffee — within hours fresh red-amber drifts to dark brown that's harder to lift. Same one-round home ceiling as red-wine-wool.
whyThisWorks: Cold press-and-blot extracts fresh tea before it locks in. A wool specialist can then use cold detergent plus a timed mild acid plus dilute peroxide to clear residual tannin without felting. Home protocol stays at one round: cold water plus wool-safe detergent plus a brief 60-second vinegar dwell, then stop.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): tea is theaflavins + thearubigins (oxidized catechin polymers) plus tannin polyphenols. Tannin anchors to wool keratin via hydrogen bonding. Pro uses cold NSD + acetic acid (60-sec dwell) + dilute peroxide for residual chromophore.
defaultAssumption: Wool + black tea = pro within hours. ONE careful home round. Speed matters more than usual because black tea oxidizes fast.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Press cold water on the BACK with vertical pressure. No rubbing — agitation felts wool. Replace cloth as it picks up brown.
Cold NSD sponge press. Suspends remaining tannin without alkalinity that weakens wool.
Rinse until water runs clear. Residual NSD interferes with later steps.
Diluted acetic acid via dropper for residual color. STRICT 60-sec dwell. Flush cold immediately.

spottingProtocol.agent: Dilute Hydrogen Peroxide for Oxidized Residue
spottingProtocol.instruction: For oxidized brown, dilute peroxide 1–2 min monitored. Flush cold immediately.
Rinse until clear. Press between dry towels — never wring. Lay flat to air-dry, reshape damp.

steps: 5. Dilute H₂O₂ for oxidized residue, 1–2 min monitored.
6. Final cold rinse, press flat to air-dry.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — wool felts above 30°C when wet
warnings: NEVER hot water — felts wool + sets tannin
NEVER alkaline cleaners — felt + darken tannin
60-sec ceiling on acid; 1–2 min on peroxide
Black tea oxidizes fast — speed matters

homeSolutions.agent: ONE Round Max — Speed Matters on Black Tea
homeSolutions.instruction: Black tea oxidizes faster than coffee — within hours, fresh amber drifts to dark brown that's much harder to lift. Wool's failure modes (heat felts + sets, alkaline felts + darkens, agitation felts) stack with tea's fast oxidation. The one-round home rule on wool is non-negotiable. Move fast, careful, then stop.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Press-Blot from the BACK
homeSolutions.instruction: Turn inside out. Place white towel under the stain. Press a SECOND white cloth dampened in COLD/ice water onto the BACK. Vertical pressure only — no rubbing. Cold ONLY — heat felts wool AND sets tea tannin.

Half teaspoon Eucalan or Soak in two cups COLD water. Dip cloth, wring nearly dry, PRESS on stain. 3 minutes dwell. ONE PASS. Do NOT use OxiClean — alkaline opens wool scales AND turns tea tannin permanently darker.

Mix 1 part white vinegar with 1 part cool water. Press a cotton swab on the stain for 60 SECONDS MAX — set a timer. Flush with cool water immediately. Vinegar handles residual tannin AND keeps it on the lighter (acid) side of the color equilibrium.

Press wool between two dry white towels — stack, press, lift. No wringing or twisting. Lay flat on fresh towel, reshape damp, air-dry at room temperature out of sun. NEVER hang wet wool. NEVER apply heat.

homeSolutions.agent: Pro Within Hours If Anything Remains
homeSolutions.instruction: Take to a wool specialist (call ahead). Tell them: "Black tea on wool, age [hours], one round at home with cold water + Eucalan + 60-sec vinegar. No hot water, no enzymes, no OxiClean. [Residual brown / fully clear]."

steps: 1. Wool + black tea = ONE home round max. Move fast.
2. Cold water press-blot from the BACK (no rubbing).
3. One pass with Eucalan / Soak, 3 min cold.
5. Press dry between towels, lay flat air-dry.
6. Anything remaining = pro. NO second home round.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop after one careful round. Repeat home chemistry on wool felts the fiber.
warnings: NEVER hot water — felts wool + sets tannin
NEVER enzymes / OxiClean / alkaline — darkens tea tannin permanently

neverDo: Never agitate or scrub wet wool
Never OxiClean / alkaline — darkens tannin AND felts wool
fiberSensitivities: Wool keratin felts when wet + warm + agitated
fiber bonds re-lock together irreversibly under heat
Alkaline opens scales AND locks tannin in dark form
Black tea oxidizes faster than coffee — speed matters

materialWarnings: ONE round home max — repeat chemistry felts wool
Black tea oxidizes fast — speed matters

escalation.when: After one careful home round, regardless of result. Earlier for chai with milk, large spills, or high-value garments.
escalation.whatToTell: "Black tea on wool, age [hours], one round at home with cold water + [wool-safe detergent] + 60-sec vinegar. No hot water, no enzymes, no OxiClean. [Residual brown / fully clear]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner specializing in wool (call ahead)

contextQuestions: Black tea (this card) or chai with milk?
How long ago — fresh, hours, or older (oxidized)?
Wool type — merino / regular / felt / blend?
Has anyone used hot water / OxiClean / agitation?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Black tea has the highest tannin load — tougher than green or herbal. Chai with milk adds milk protein — earlier escalation. Aged tea (4+ hours) drifts brown and needs reducing bleach (pro-only on wool).
